title: Variance of a Sum: The Cross Term

beats.prompt: Linearity of expectation added with no fine print. Match each item to its value.

pairs.left: Var of ONE fair die (from L1)

feedback.correct: Expectation adds unconditionally; one die has Var=35/12 (L1).
hints: One is the EV-L2 unconditional rule, one is L1's die variance.
Linearity never needs independence; Var=91/6−49/4.
Match: 'Linearity: E[X+Y]=…' → 'E[X]+E[Y], always'; 'Var of ONE fair die' → '35/12'.

beats.prompt: Linearity of expectation (EV L2) added with no fine print — E[X+Y]=E[X]+E[Y], dependent or not. Does the *variance* of a sum add just as cleanly? Two independent fair dice, X₁ and X₂: is Var(X₁+X₂) equal to Var(X₁)+Var(X₂)?

options: Here yes, because these dice are independent (Cov=0)

Always, just like expectation adds.note: Linearity adds with no fine print, but variance picks up a 2Cov(X,Y) cross term. It matches the naive sum only when Cov=0 — true here, but for a reason, not by default.

Never — variances can't be added.note: Too pessimistic. Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y)+2Cov always holds; independent ⇒ cross term 0 ⇒ they add to 35/6 (#2).

Here yes, because these dice are indepen.note: The cross term is 0 only because of independence. 35/12+35/12=35/6 (#2).

beats.prompt: Drag the Cov slider. Watch how the variance of the sum changes relative to the sum of variances. The gap between the bars is exactly 2·Cov. Drag to Cov=0 — the bars snap flush.

feedback.correct: At Cov=0 the bars are flush: Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y). Drag ± and the right bar over/undershoots by exactly 2·Cov.
hints: Drag the slider to Cov=0 — notice the bars are flush (gap=0).
Drag positive: the right bar (Var(X+Y)) grows beyond the left by 2·Cov.
Drag negative: the right bar shrinks. The gap is always labeled '= 2·Cov'.

beats.prompt: Quick reminder before the mastery challenge — read the card, then continue.

interaction.title: Covariance is bilinear
interaction.body:
Covariance splits over sums in each argument:
  Cov(X, Y+Z) = Cov(X,Y) + Cov(X,Z)
  Cov(X+W, Y) = Cov(X,Y) + Cov(W,Y)

A key special case: Cov(X,X) = Var(X). A variable always covaries perfectly with itself — that term never vanishes, even when the two outer variables are independent.

feedback.correct: Covariance is bilinear: it splits over sums. Cov(X,X)=Var(X) always.
hints: Read the card.
Cov splits over sums in each slot.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Two independent fair dice X₁ and X₂. Their individual variances are each 35/12 (L1), and they are independent so Cov(X₁,X₂)=0. What is Var(X₁+X₂)? Enter as a fraction.

feedback.correct: Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y)+2Cov; independence kills the cross term, so 35/12+35/12=35/6.
hints: Sum's variance = two variances + cross term; recall Cov for *independent* dice.
Each Var=35/12; Cov=0 drops 2Cov; add — don't report one, don't multiply.

beats.prompt: You just computed it. Now name the rule.

lenses.label: Formula
lenses.body: Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) + 2·Cov(X,Y). The 2Cov term is the cross term — it measures how much the variables move together and corrects the naive sum.

lenses.label: When cross term vanishes
lenses.body: Cov(X,Y)=0 when X and Y are independent. Then Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y) — variances add cleanly, just like expectations. But this is a special case, not the default.

lenses.label: Contrast with linearity
lenses.body: E[X+Y]=E[X]+E[Y] holds always — no fine print. Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y) holds only when Cov=0. Expectation is unconditionally linear; variance is not.

feedback.correct: Variances add cleanly only when Cov=0. The 2Cov cross term is the correction.
hints: Tap each lens card to reveal.
The formula vs the linearity lens show the key contrast.
Tap all three cards.

beats.prompt: Transfer challenge: two independent fair dice X₁ and X₂, each with variance 35/12. Using bilinearity, find Cov(2X₁, X₁+X₂) — the covariance of a DOUBLED component with the sum. Enter as a fraction.

interaction.scenario: A scaled component still covaries with the whole. Split Cov(2X₁, X₁+X₂) with bilinearity — the constant 2 pulls out of the covariance — before reaching for 'independent so 0'. Recall Cov(X₁,X₁)=Var(X₁)=35/12 and Cov(X₁,X₂)=0.

feedback.correct: Cov(2X₁,X₁+X₂)=2·Cov(X₁,X₁)+2·Cov(X₁,X₂)=2·Var(X₁)+0=2·(35/12)=35/6. The constant scales straight through, and the self-covariance term Var(X₁) is what survives — independence only kills Cov(X₁,X₂).
hints: Pull the 2 out, then split over the sum: Cov(2X₁,X₁+X₂)=2[Cov(X₁,X₁)+Cov(X₁,X₂)].
Cov(X₁,X₁)=Var(X₁)=35/12 survives; Cov(X₁,X₂)=0 by independence. So you have 2·(35/12+0).

beats.interviewNote: Interview framing: Cov(X₁,S) where S=X₁+X₂ is the canonical example of 'the part correlates with the whole.' The bilinearity split — Cov(X₁,X₁)+Cov(X₁,X₂)=Var(X₁)+0=35/12 — is the exact move that re-appears in portfolio variance decomposition (β·Var(B)) and in any regression where a component also appears in the target. The sharp diagnostic distractor is 0: learners who think 'X₁ and X₂ are independent so every covariance is 0' forget that S is not a stranger to X₁ — it literally contains it.
beats.prompt: Let S = X₁+X₂ (sum of two independent fair dice). What is Cov(X₁, S)? Use bilinearity to split the covariance, then simplify. Enter as a fraction.

interaction.scenario: S contains X₁, so even though X₁ and X₂ are independent, S is not independent of X₁. Split Cov(X₁, X₁+X₂) before reaching for 'independent so 0'.

feedback.correct: Cov(X₁,S)=Cov(X₁,X₁)+Cov(X₁,X₂)=Var(X₁)+0=35/12 — the part correlates with the whole because the whole contains it.
hints: S contains X₁; split Cov(X₁,X₁+X₂) before reaching for 'independent so 0'.
=Var(X₁)+Cov(X₁,X₂); dice independent ⇒ Cov(X₁,X₂)=0 but Cov(X₁,X₁)=Var(X₁)=35/12 survives.
35/12 (#3). X₁,X₂ are independent, but S isn't a stranger to X₁ — it contains it. Cov(X₁,X₁)+Cov(X₁,X₂)=Var(X₁)+0=35/12. A variable always covaries perfectly with itself; that term never vanishes.

beats.prompt: The moves that ran through this lesson.

feedback.correct: Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y)+2Cov(X,Y). Variances add cleanly only when Cov=0; the cross term is the correction. Covariance is bilinear: Cov(X,X)=Var(X) never vanishes. Next: a unit-free score.
hints: Read each recap card.
The key contrast: E[X+Y] adds always; Var(X+Y) adds cleanly only when Cov=0.
Tap to continue.
